perturbation.original_text: 5.  Right to Audit 5.1. The Town retains the right to audit the Provider to ensure that proper measures are being applied to protect any and all confidential information. Auditing may be performed by Town IT Staff or by a Third Par ty, as chosen by the Town in its sole discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.  Product of this Agreement 9.1. Any new information or know ledge generated from the discussions to be carried out as a result of this Agreement may not be divulged to others in verbal or written or any other form without the express written consent of the Town . 5.1. The Town retains the right to audit the Provider to ensure that proper measures are being applied to protect any and all confidential information. Auditing may be performed by Town IT Staff or by a Third Par ty, as chosen by the Town in its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the right to audit into the 'Product of this Agreement' section makes it seem like it only applies to new information generated, not all confidential information, reducing its scope and enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: MFIPPA mandates that municipalities protect the privacy of information they hold. By obscuring the Town's right to audit the Provider's security measures, the modification weakens the Town's ability to ensure compliance with MFIPPA.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by moving the audit clause into a section concerning 'Product of this Agreement,' limiting its applicability to only new information or knowledge generated. This weakens the Town's ability to audit the Provider's security measures for *all* confidential information as initially stated. The Ministry of Correctional Services Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. M.22 states in Section 10 'Confidentiality'. By limiting the audit scope, the modification contradicts the intent to protect confidentiality broadly, as placing a required legal disclosure in an irrelevant section makes it difficult for parties to locate or interpret, which falls under Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction.
